schools.summary: The Lamb and his redeemed: 'and I looked, and, lo, a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand, having his Father's name written in their foreheads' — over against the beast and its worshippers (ch. 13), the Lamb (Christ) seen standing on Mount Zion with his redeemed company (the vision turning from the persecuting power to the triumphant Lamb and his faithful, Mount Zion signifying the church, the place of God's people, or the heavenly Zion); the '144,000' (the sealed of 7:4) the Lamb's faithful company (whether the same sealed multitude or the complete number of the redeemed, the symbolic number marking the full company of God's people preserved through the beast's persecution); 'his Father's name written in their foreheads' the mark of God's ownership (the seal of 7:3), in pointed contrast to the mark of the beast (13:16), the redeemed openly sealed as his (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Victorinus and Bede: the Lamb on Mount Zion with the sealed company is Christ with his church; the Father's name written on their foreheads marks them as God's own, sealed and owned by him in deliberate contrast to those who bear the mark of the beast — the redeemed company gathered with the Lamb, secured by the divine seal through the persecution of the antichristian power.

themes: a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand
having his Father's name written in their foreheads
the Lamb on Mount Zion with the 144,000
the redeemed sealed with God's name, over against the beast's mark

schools.summary: The mighty heavenly sound: 'and I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps' — a great voice from heaven, like the roar of many waters and a great thunder, yet also like the sweet sound of harpers playing (the combination of majesty, waters and thunder, and melody, harps, in the heavenly song); 'as many waters... a great thunder' marking the volume, power, and majesty of the heavenly sound (overwhelming and grand, the vast company's song mighty as the sea and the storm); 'harpers harping with their harps' marking that the sound, though mighty, was also musical and sweet, like skilled harpers (the heavenly worship combining power and beauty, the redeemed singing to the accompaniment of harps, cf. 5:8; 15:2) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder
the voice of harpers harping with their harps
the great voice from heaven and the harpers
the heavenly worship mighty in majesty and sweet in melody

schools.summary: The new song of the redeemed: 'and they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts (living creatures), and the elders: and no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth' — the 144,000 singing a new song before God's throne, the living creatures, and the elders (a 'new song,' 5:9, befitting the new, fuller experience of redemption, the song of the redeemed sung in the heavenly court, celebrating the new and greater work of grace, expressing the joy and gratitude of those saved by the Lamb); 'no man could learn that song but the 144,000, which were redeemed from the earth' — only the redeemed able to learn or sing this song (the experience of redemption its prerequisite, those who have not been redeemed unable to enter into its meaning or joy, the song belonging uniquely to the ransomed company) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts, and the elders
no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth
the new song that only the redeemed could learn
the song of redemption belonging uniquely to the ransomed

schools.summary: The purity and devotion of the redeemed: 'these are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins. These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth. These were redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb' — 'not defiled with women; for they are virgins (parthenoi)' most often taken figuratively (the chastity spiritual: the redeemed undefiled by the spiritual fornication of idolatry and apostasy, the beast-worship of ch. 13, keeping themselves pure for Christ, the figure of the church as a chaste virgin, 2 Corinthians 11:2; a minority taking it of literal celibacy/virginity, but the dominant reading spiritual purity from idolatry); 'follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth' marking their unreserved devotion to Christ (wholly devoted, following him in obedience, suffering, and glory, the mark of the true disciple); 'redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb' marking their consecration (ransomed out of mankind and set apart as holy to God, whether the first and choicest of the redeemed or the whole harvest consecrated to God) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: these are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins
these are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth
the purity of the redeemed who follow the Lamb
spiritual purity from idolatry, devotion, and consecration as firstfruits

schools.summary: The blamelessness of the redeemed: 'and in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God' — the redeemed marked by truthfulness (no guile/falsehood in their mouth) and blamelessness (without fault), sincere and pure, fit to stand before God's throne; 'no guile (no lie, no deceit) in their mouth' marking their truthfulness and integrity (in contrast to the lies of the beast and false prophet, ch. 13, the redeemed sincere, holding and speaking the truth, the mark of those who belong to the God of truth, cf. Zephaniah 3:13; Psalm 32:2); 'without fault (amomoi — blameless, faultless) before the throne of God' marking their being accounted blameless in God's presence (whether by their consecrated lives or, more deeply, by the righteousness of the Lamb that makes them faultless before God, the redeemed standing unblemished before the throne, the perfected company of the saved) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God
the blamelessness of the redeemed
truthful, sincere, and blameless before God
the perfected company standing unblemished in God's presence

schools.summary: The angel with the everlasting gospel: 'and I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people' — an angel flying in mid-heaven (the zenith, visible to all), bearing the everlasting gospel (eternal good news) to proclaim, the first of three angelic messages (the gospel proclaimed in the face of the beast's dominion); the 'everlasting gospel' the eternal, unchanging good news of God (the abiding message of salvation, its proclamation to all the earth even in the time of the beast marking God's continued call to repentance and faith); proclaimed 'to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people' (the fourfold designation marking its universal proclamation to all mankind, the everlasting gospel for the whole world, God's saving message reaching every people even under the beast's sway) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Ellicott.

themes: another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach
to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people
the angel with the everlasting gospel for all nations
God's eternal saving message proclaimed to all the world

schools.summary: The angel's proclamation: 'saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters' — the everlasting gospel's call to fear and glorify God (over against the worship of the beast, the angel summoning all to reverence and honor the true God, the call to true worship in the face of false); 'the hour of his judgment is come' the ground of the summons (the time of God's judgment arrived, the call to fear and worship urged by the nearness of judgment, the warning lending urgency to the gospel-summons, men called to turn to God for judgment is at hand); 'worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters' the call to worship the Creator (the true object of worship the Creator of all, over against the beast and its image, men called to worship the God who made all things, the appeal to God as Creator grounding the duty of worshiping him alone) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come
worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters
the angel's call to fear God, for the hour of judgment is come
the call to worship the Creator over against the beast

schools.summary: The second angel and the fall of Babylon: 'and there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication' — the second angel announcing the fall of Babylon (the repetition 'is fallen, is fallen' marking the certainty and completeness of her ruin, drawn from Isaiah 21:9; Jeremiah 51:8); 'that great city' the great antichristian power named Babylon (the symbolic name for the great corrupt and persecuting power, described fully in chs. 17-18, the historicists reading it of papal Rome, the great city on seven hills, 17:9, the apostate religious power, others of pagan Rome or the world-system, the name 'Babylon' marking her as the seat of idolatry, corruption, and opposition to God's people); 'she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication' the cause of her fall (she seduced the nations into her spiritual fornication — idolatry, corruption, false worship — intoxicating them with her corrupting influence, her fall the judgment for her seduction of the nations) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city
she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication
the second angel proclaiming Babylon's fall
the great antichristian power doomed for seducing the nations

schools.summary: The third angel's warning: 'and the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand' — the third angel uttering a solemn warning against beast-worship (the condition stated — worshiping the beast and image, receiving the mark — before the dreadful consequence, vv.10-11, is declared); the warning concerning those who 'worship the beast and his image' (the idolatry of the antichristian power, ch. 13, the third angel warning against the very worship the beast and false prophet enforce, the choice set before all: worship the beast, or fear God, v.7); receiving 'his mark in his forehead, or in his hand' (13:16) marking open allegiance to the beast (the warning against taking the mark of subjection to the antichristian power, the solemn condition preparing for the warning of wrath against all who so submit) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand
the third angel's warning against worshipping the beast
the solemn condition before the warning of wrath
the choice set before all: the beast, or God

schools.summary: The wrath against beast-worshippers: 'the same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb' — the beast-worshipper shall drink God's wrath, poured undiluted (without mixture, full strength) into the cup of his indignation (the 'without mixture' marking the full, unmitigated severity of the wrath, undiluted by mercy, the figure of the cup of God's anger, Psalm 75:8; Jeremiah 25:15, the judgment unrelieved); 'tormented with fire and brimstone (the figure of dreadful, unending punishment, Sodom, Genesis 19; the lake of fire, 20:10) in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb' — the punishment open, witnessed by heaven, the most solemn warning of wrath against beast-worship (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation
tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb
the wrath of God against the worshippers of the beast
the full, unmitigated severity of the threatened judgment

schools.summary: The unending torment: 'and the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name' — the smoke of their torment rising forever, and they having no rest at all (the figure of perpetual, unceasing punishment, drawn from Isaiah 34:10; cf. Genesis 19:28, the unending and restless torment of those who worship the beast); 'for ever and ever' marking the eternal, ceaseless duration of the punishment (the rising smoke the perpetual token of the judgment, the most emphatic statement of the endless torment of the impenitent beast-worshipper); 'they have no rest day nor night' in pointed contrast to the rest of those who die in the Lord (v.13, the beast-worshippers having no respite from their torment while the faithful rest from their labors, the warning set against the blessing to follow) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night
the unending torment of the beast-worshippers
the perpetual, restless punishment of all who worship the beast
set against the rest of those who die in the Lord

schools.summary: The patience of the saints: 'Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus' — in view of the warning of wrath against beast-worship (vv.9-11), the saints' patient endurance called forth (the faithful those who keep God's commandments and the faith of Jesus, enduring rather than yielding to the beast); 'here is the patience (hypomone — patient endurance) of the saints' — the dreadful fate of beast-worshippers (and the rest of the faithful, v.13) the ground of the saints' patient endurance (sustained to endure the beast's persecution rather than receive his mark, knowing the contrast of destinies); 'they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus' the two marks of the true saint (as in 12:17: obedience to God's commandments and the holding of the faith of Jesus, those who amid the beast's pressure remain loyal to God and Christ) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus
the patience of the saints who keep God's commandments
the saints sustained to endure rather than yield to the beast
the two marks of the true saint: obedience and the faith of Jesus

schools.summary: The blessing on the faithful dead: 'and I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Write, Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth: Yea, saith the Spirit, that they may rest from their labours; and their works do follow them' — a voice from heaven bidding John write a beatitude on the faithful dead (those who die 'in the Lord,' in union with Christ, faithful unto death, pronounced blessed, the comfort to the persecuted saints that to die in the Lord is gain, 'from henceforth' marking especially those who die under the beast's persecution); 'that they may rest from their labours' the Spirit's confirmation (the faithful dead resting from their labors and sufferings, in pointed contrast to the beast-worshippers who have 'no rest day nor night,' v.11, the toil and persecution over, rest begun); 'their works do follow them' the abiding fruit (their good works accompanying them, not as the ground of salvation but as its evidence and reward, the fruit of their faith following them into God's presence) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Ellicott.

themes: Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth
that they may rest from their labours; and their works do follow them
the blessing on the dead who die in the Lord
the faithful dead resting, their works their abiding fruit

schools.summary: The Son of man come to reap: 'and I looked, and behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle' — one like the Son of man (Christ, Daniel 7:13) seen sitting on a white cloud (the figure of Christ coming to reap, the white cloud marking glory and the coming of the Lord, cf. 1:7; Matthew 24:30); the 'golden crown (stephanos)' marking his royal authority and victory (the reaper the crowned King, coming in his sovereignty to gather the harvest of the earth); 'a sharp sickle' the instrument of reaping (Christ coming to reap the harvest of the earth, whether the harvest the gathering of the elect or the judgment of the world, vv.15-16, the sharp sickle ready for the appointed reaping) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man
having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle
the Son of man on the cloud with crown and sickle
the crowned King come to reap the harvest of the earth

schools.summary: The call to reap: 'and another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap; for the harvest of the earth is ripe' — an angel coming from the temple (from God's presence) and crying to the Son of man to thrust in the sickle and reap (the command for the harvest issuing from the temple, by God's appointment); 'the time is come for thee to reap' the appointed hour of harvest arrived (the reaping at God's appointed time, the angel announcing that the moment has come); 'the harvest of the earth is ripe (exeranthe — dried, fully ripe)' the harvest ready (whether the ripening of the elect for gathering, or, with the vintage, vv.18-20, the ripening of the wicked for judgment, the figure of the final ingathering/judgment at the appointed time) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap
the harvest of the earth is ripe
the call to reap the ripe harvest
the final ingathering commanded at God's appointed time

schools.summary: The reaping accomplished: 'and he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped' — at the angel's word (v.15), the Son of man thrusting in his sickle and the earth being reaped (the harvest accomplished, the appointed ingathering carried out swiftly and completely); 'thrust in his sickle on the earth' the reaping done by Christ himself or his agency (the figure of the decisive, appointed harvest of the earth at the end); 'the earth was reaped' the harvest complete (whether the gathering of the elect, the wheat into the barn, Matthew 13:30, or the harvest of judgment, the reaping of the earth finished at Christ's hand, the appointed ingathering accomplished) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped
the reaping of the earth
the appointed harvest accomplished at Christ's hand
the ingathering of the earth complete

schools.summary: The angel of the vintage: 'and another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle' — a second angel coming from the heavenly temple, also bearing a sharp sickle (this angel to gather the vintage, vv.18-20, as the Son of man reaped the harvest, vv.14-16, the second reaping, of the grapes, now introduced); the angel coming 'out of the temple which is in heaven' (from God's presence, the command and agency of the vintage proceeding from God, the heavenly temple the source of the appointed judgment); 'he also having a sharp sickle' (like the Son of man, v.14, but now for the gathering of the grapes, the sharp sickle marking readiness for the vintage of wrath, the instrument of the coming judgment on the wicked) — Barnes, Robertson.

themes: another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle
the angel with a sharp sickle for the vintage
the second reaping, of the grapes, introduced
the appointed agent of the vintage of wrath

schools.summary: The call for the vintage: 'and another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire; and cried with a loud cry to him that had the sharp sickle, saying, Thrust in thy sharp sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth; for her grapes are fully ripe' — an angel coming from the altar (the altar of incense, associated with the prayers of the saints, 6:9-10; 8:3-5), having power over fire (the call for the vintage coming from the altar, suggesting the judgment answers the prayers of the saints for vindication); the angel bidding the angel with the sickle gather the grape-clusters of the earth's vine (the 'vine of the earth' the wicked, ripe for judgment, in contrast to the true Vine, Christ, the gathering of the grapes for the winepress of wrath); 'her grapes are fully ripe' the wickedness of the earth fully ripe, ready for judgment (the iniquity of the world come to its full measure, cf. Genesis 15:16; Joel 3:13, the vintage of wrath due) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: Thrust in thy sharp sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth; for her grapes are fully ripe
another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire
the call to gather the ripe grapes of the earth
the wicked, ripe in iniquity, gathered for the winepress of wrath

schools.summary: The vintage cast into the winepress: 'and the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth, and cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God' — the angel reaping the earth's vine (the wicked) and casting it into the great winepress of God's wrath (the figure of the gathering of the wicked for the execution of judgment, the winepress where the grapes are trodden the place of wrath); 'the vine of the earth' the wicked of the earth, ripe in iniquity, gathered for judgment (the harvest of the ungodly, in contrast to the ingathering of the elect, v.16); 'the great winepress of the wrath of God' the figure of God's judgment (Isaiah 63:1-6; Joel 3:13, where the wicked are trodden as grapes, the 'great winepress' marking the severity and finality of the judgment on the ungodly) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the angel thrust in his sickle into the earth, and gathered the vine of the earth
cast it into the great winepress of the wrath of God
the vintage cast into the winepress of wrath
the wicked gathered to be trodden in the winepress of judgment

schools.summary: The treading of the winepress: 'and the winepress was trodden without the city, and blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles, by the space of a thousand and six hundred furlongs' — the winepress of God's wrath trodden outside the city (the treading, Isaiah 63:3, the act of judgment, crushing the wicked as grapes, 'without the city' perhaps marking the judgment as outside the holy city, or the place of execution); 'blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles' the figure of immense bloodshed (the treading producing blood, not wine, rising to the horses' bridles, the hyperbolic figure of an enormous, terrible slaughter, the vast extent of the judgment on the wicked); 'a thousand and six hundred furlongs' (stadia, about 200 miles) the immense distance marking the vast, widespread extent of the judgment (whether a symbolic number, perhaps 4×4×100, the four quarters of the earth, or a figure for the whole land, the universality and magnitude of the judgment on the ungodly) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the winepress was trodden without the city, and blood came out of the winepress, even unto the horse bridles
by the space of a thousand and six hundred furlongs
the winepress trodden, blood to the horse bridles
the enormous, widespread judgment on the wicked
